content.text: A resource group is a logical container within a subscription that holds related Azure resources. Every resource in Azure must belong to exactly one resource group, and the resource group determines the scope for access control, cost management, and lifecycle operations. When you delete a resource group, all resources inside it are deleted together — making resource groups the primary unit of lifecycle management in Azure.

Resource groups are free — there is no charge for creating them. They exist only at the subscription level (not nested inside other resource groups) and can contain resources from multiple Azure regions.

attrs.alt: Resource group organizational strategies with trade-offs

content.text:
# Basic creation
az group create --name myRG --location eastus

# With tags
az group create   --name myapp-prod-compute   --location eastus   --tags environment=production application=myapp team=platform cost-center=cc-1042

# Verify creation
az group show --name myapp-prod-compute --output table

# List all resource groups in the subscription
az group list --output table

# List groups matching a pattern
az group list --query "[?starts_with(name, 'myapp')]" --output table

# Create a resource group
New-AzResourceGroup -Name myRG -Location eastus

# With tags
New-AzResourceGroup `
  -Name myapp-prod-compute `
  -Location eastus `
  -Tag @{environment="production"; application="myapp"; team="platform"}

# Verify
Get-AzResourceGroup -Name myapp-prod-compute | Select-Object ResourceGroupName, Location, ProvisioningState

# List groups
Get-AzResourceGroup | Select-Object ResourceGroupName, Location | Sort-Object ResourceGroupName

# Deploy the subscription-scope Bicep
az deployment sub create   --location eastus   --template-file main.bicep   --parameters environment=prod application=myapp

Resource group names must be 1–90 characters, unique within the subscription, and can include alphanumerics, underscores, parentheses, hyphens, and periods (but not end with a period). A consistent naming convention makes groups self-documenting and enables scripted operations using name patterns.

Choose a pattern that encodes the information you most frequently filter by. If you often list groups by team, put team first. If you filter by environment, put environment first. Consistency matters more than the specific pattern chosen.

# Convention: {app}-{env}-{tier}
# Create all resource groups for a new application
APP="myapp"
LOCATION="eastus"

for env in dev staging prod; do
    for tier in networking compute data; do
        NAME="${APP}-${env}-${tier}"
        az group create           --name "$NAME"           --location "$LOCATION"           --tags application="$APP" environment="$env" tier="$tier"
        echo "Created: $NAME"
    done
done

A resource group has a location (region) that determines where its metadata is stored — not where the resources inside it must be deployed. Resources in a resource group can be in any region. The resource group location matters for disaster recovery: if the resource group's metadata region is unavailable, ARM cannot process management operations for that group even if the resources themselves are running in a different region.

# Resource group in eastus can contain resources in westus and northeurope
az group create --name myRG --location eastus

az vm create   --resource-group myRG   --name vm-eastus   --location eastus   --image Ubuntu2204   --size Standard_B1s   --generate-ssh-keys

# This VM is in westeurope but the resource GROUP is in eastus
# (its metadata is stored in eastus)
az vm create   --resource-group myRG   --name vm-westeurope   --location westeurope   --image Ubuntu2204   --size Standard_B1s   --generate-ssh-keys

Tags applied to a resource group do not automatically propagate to the resources inside it. Each resource carries its own tags independently. Azure Policy can enforce tag inheritance (automatically copying RG tags to child resources) if consistent tagging across a group is required.

# Set tags (replaces all existing tags)
az group update   --name myRG   --set tags.environment=production tags.owner=platform-team

# Add a tag without replacing existing ones
az tag update   --resource-id $(az group show --name myRG --query id -o tsv)   --operation Merge   --tags cost-center=cc-1042

# Remove a specific tag
az tag update   --resource-id $(az group show --name myRG --query id -o tsv)   --operation Delete   --tags old-tag=""

# List all resource groups with a specific tag value
az group list   --query "[?tags.environment=='production'].{Name:name, Location:location}"   --output table
Enforcing Tag Inheritance with Azure Policy

# Assign a built-in policy that copies RG tags to child resources
# Policy: "Inherit a tag from the resource group if missing"
az policy assignment create   --name "inherit-environment-tag"   --scope /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}   --policy /providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/ea3f2387-9b95-492a-a190-fcdc54f7b070   --params '{"tagName": {"value": "environment"}}'   --identity-type SystemAssigned   --location eastus

# The policy assigns the resource group's 'environment' tag to any resource
# in that group that does not already have the tag — applied on create/update

Resources can be moved between resource groups (and even between subscriptions) after creation. Not all resource types support move operations, and some resources have restrictions (for example, a VM with disk encryption cannot be moved while encrypted). The move validates first in a dry run, then commits.

# Check if a resource type supports move
az resource list   --resource-group mySourceRG   --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type}"   --output table

# Validate the move first (dry-run)
az resource invoke-action   --action validateMoveResources   --resource-id /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/mySourceRG   --request-body '{
    "resources": [
      "/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/mySourceRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa"
    ],
    "targetResourceGroup": "/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myDestRG"
  }'

# Move a specific resource to a different resource group
az resource move   --destination-group myDestRG   --ids /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/mySourceRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa

# Move multiple resources at once
az resource move   --destination-group myDestRG   --ids     /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/mySourceRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa     /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/mySourceRG/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/myapp
App Service Plan must move with the apps
Disks, NICs, and VNets in same move operation
No peerings active; no linked gateways
Delete backup data first; then recreate

Deleting a resource group deletes all resources inside it in parallel. This is the fastest way to clean up an entire environment. The delete operation is asynchronous — ARM returns 202 and you poll until all resources are removed.

# Delete a resource group and all resources in it
az group delete --name myRG --yes --no-wait

# Wait for deletion to complete
az group wait --name myRG --deleted
echo "Resource group deleted"

# Delete multiple groups (e.g. all dev groups for an app)
az group list   --query "[?tags.environment=='dev' && tags.application=='myapp'].name"   --output tsv | while read rg; do
    echo "Deleting: $rg"
    az group delete --name "$rg" --yes --no-wait
  done

# Wait for all deletions
az group list   --query "[?tags.environment=='dev' && tags.application=='myapp'].name"   --output tsv | while read rg; do
    az group wait --name "$rg" --deleted 2>/dev/null
    echo "Deleted: $rg"
  done

Resource group deletion cannot be undone. Use az lock create --lock-type CanNotDelete on production resource groups to prevent accidental deletion. The lock blocks delete operations even for Owners until the lock is explicitly removed.

# Protect a production resource group from accidental deletion
az lock create   --name protect-prod   --lock-type CanNotDelete   --resource-group myapp-prod-compute   --notes "Production resource group — removal requires explicit lock delete"

# List locks on a group
az lock list --resource-group myapp-prod-compute --output table

# Remove the lock before deletion (requires Owner role or User Access Administrator)
az lock delete --name protect-prod --resource-group myapp-prod-compute
Support ticket can increase this limit
Per type, not total; practical limit much higher
Must be unique within the subscription

Resource groups are a natural RBAC scope boundary. Assigning a role at the resource group scope grants the same permission on every resource inside the group, and the assignment is inherited by all resources.

# Grant a user Contributor on a specific resource group only
az role assignment create \
  --assignee user@example.com \
  --role Contributor \
  --resource-group myapp-dev-compute

# Grant a service principal Reader
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <service-principal-object-id> \
  --role Reader \
  --scope /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myapp-prod-compute

# List all role assignments on a resource group
az role assignment list \
  --resource-group myapp-dev-compute \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[].{Principal:principalName, Role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  --output table

#!/bin/bash
# Dev team: Contributor on dev groups only
DEV_GROUP_OID='<dev-team-aad-group-oid>'
for tier in networking compute data; do
    az role assignment create \
      --assignee "$DEV_GROUP_OID" \
      --role Contributor \
      --resource-group "myapp-dev-${tier}"
done

# Ops team: Contributor on prod groups
OPS_GROUP_OID='<ops-team-aad-group-oid>'
for tier in networking compute data; do
    az role assignment create \
      --assignee "$OPS_GROUP_OID" \
      --role Contributor \
      --resource-group "myapp-prod-${tier}"
done

# Dev team: Reader on prod for observability
for tier in networking compute data; do
    az role assignment create \
      --assignee "$DEV_GROUP_OID" \
      --role Reader \
      --resource-group "myapp-prod-${tier}"
done
The Azure SDK for Python's ResourceManagementClient handles resource group CRUD and is the right tool for automation scripts that create environments programmatically.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.resources.models import ResourceGroup

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, '00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000')

# Create a resource group
rg = client.resource_groups.create_or_update(
    resource_group_name='myapp-dev-compute',
    parameters=ResourceGroup(
        location='eastus',
        tags={
            'application': 'myapp',
            'environment': 'dev',
            'tier': 'compute',
        }
    )
)
print(f'Created: {rg.name} ({rg.provisioning_state})')

# List all resource groups
for group in client.resource_groups.list():
    print(f'  {group.name} -- {group.location} -- {group.tags}')

# Check existence
exists = client.resource_groups.check_existence('myapp-dev-compute')
print(f'Exists: {exists}')

# Delete (long-running operation)
poller = client.resource_groups.begin_delete('myapp-dev-compute')
poller.wait()
print('Resource group deleted')
Auditing Resource Groups with Azure Resource Graph
Azure Resource Graph lets you query resource group metadata at scale across subscriptions without iterating through az group list calls. It uses KQL and returns results in seconds.

# Query resource groups via Azure Resource Graph
az graph query -q '
ResourceContainers
| where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
| project name, location, tags
| order by name asc
' --output table

# Find resource groups missing a required tag
az graph query -q '
ResourceContainers
| where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
| where isnull(tags["cost-center"])
| project name, location
' --output table

# Count resource groups per environment tag
az graph query -q '
ResourceContainers
| where type == "microsoft.resources/subscriptions/resourcegroups"
| summarize count() by tostring(tags["environment"])
| order by count_ desc
' --output table
A common pattern for large subscriptions is a shared Bicep module that creates a resource group with standard tags, locks, and RBAC assignments — enforcing organizational standards in one place.

Resource groups are the primary lifecycle and access-control unit in Azure. Create them with az group create or in Bicep at subscription scope. Choose a naming convention that encodes environment, application, and tier so groups are self-documenting and scriptable. The group's location stores metadata — resources inside can span any region. Tags on groups do not auto-propagate to children without Azure Policy. Delete a group with az group delete to remove all contained resources at once, and protect production groups with CanNotDelete locks.
